does: This paper investigates the internal mechanisms of reward models used in reinforcement learning from human feedback, specifically examining how helpfulness and harmlessness objectives interact within the model. The authors train reward models under three conditions—helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective settings—and observe that mixed-objective models frequently underperform single-objective variants. Using activation-based analysis and targeted ablations, they identify specific neurons responsible for each objective and demonstrate through causal intervention that these neurons support their corresponding objectives while often harming performance on the opposing one. A key finding is that substantial neuron overlap exists between the two objectives, with shared neurons disproportionately influencing overall model behavior and driving alignment tension.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining reward models as core components of RLHF systems for language model alignment. It extends recent mechanistic interpretability research that applies neuron-level analysis and causal ablation techniques to understand deep learning models. The paper responds to empirical observations in alignment research showing that jointly optimizing multiple objectives can produce suboptimal results, echoing broader machine learning findings about multitask learning interference. It connects to work studying how neural networks represent conflicting objectives and builds on activation analysis methods from recent interpretability studies that probe internal model representations to understand decision-making processes and identify causal contributions of model components.
enables: These mechanistic findings open pathways for improving RLHF systems by informing better reward model architectures and training procedures that mitigate objective interference. Understanding that shared neurons drive alignment tension suggests potential interventions—such as modular architectures or training procedures that better isolate objective representations—could improve multi-objective alignment. The causal analysis methodology demonstrated here provides tools for future work investigating other conflicting objectives in language model alignment, such as truthfulness versus helpfulness or different safety considerations. These insights could guide more sophisticated reward model design and contribute to developing more robust alignment techniques as language models tackle increasingly complex multi-faceted objectives.
